source_texts: She occupied, in the Boulevard Haussmann, the second storey of a large new house, the owner of which was content to let to single ladies, in order to get his plaster dried.

The famous doctor said his goodbyes with a serious expression, but one that hadn’t given up hope. And to the timid question Ivan Ilyich put to him, raising eyes that were shining with fear and hope—is there any possibility of recovery?—he answered that though one couldn’t guarantee it, there was a possibility. The look of hope with which Ivan Ilyich said goodbye to the doctor was so pitiful that, when she saw it, Praskovya Fyodorovna burst into tears as she went through the study doors to give the famous doctor his fee.

The torso of a capripede caryatid. A carriage drawn by a pair of steeds flew up to the carriage porch, and a lackey, tricorne on his head, gave the handle a click, and the door flew open, flinging back a coat of arms adorned with tiny crowns (a unicorn goring a knight). Responding to greetings, a hand invested in leather touched the brim of a top hat.

Nevertheless La Faloise would very much have liked to have known the name of the woman at whose house the supper was to take place, but the countess had recalled Vandeuvres, and was questioning him as to the way tea was made in England. He was often there, attending the races in which his horses ran. According to him, only the Russians knew how to make tea; and he mentioned their recipe. Then, as though he had been thinking very much whilst speaking, he interrupted himself to ask, “By the way, and the marquis?

All six tiny little rooms were steam heated, as a result of which you were suffocated by moist heat in the apartment. The window panes sweated; the visitors sweated; the maid and the husband sweated; Sofia Petrovna Likhutina herself was covered with perspiration, like warm dew on a chrysanthemum. How could there possibly be any perspective?

The princesses climbed onto their royal elephants, and since Kundavai had already hinted that those leaving for a long journey must start on their journey first, Vandiya Devan turned the horse’s head towards the road albeit with great hesitation. Even then he couldn’t help but turn towards her and look upon her beautiful face once again, but realizing that it is unmannerly, he turned the horse’s head swiftly and galloped away within a flash. The Vaidiyar’s son, who was to accompany him, struggled to control his horse and followed him.

This was the philosophy worked out by the Oblomovka Plato, a philosophy which lulled him amid the issues and strict demands of duty and purpose! He had been born and reared not like a gladiator, for the arena, but as a peaceful spectator of the battle. It was not for his meek and lazy soul to endure either the alarms of happiness or life’s blows. Consequently, his person expressed one of life’s extremes, and he did nothing to achieve, change, or repent.

The officer’s comrades noticed in that ‘neglected’ garden, behind that sorry-looking rococo gate, quite a pretty creature who was almost always there when the handsome lieutenant went by. This lieutenant is not unknown to the reader: his name was Théodule Gillenormand.

“Ah!” he exclaimed, interrupting himself. “There come Mignon and Steiner! They are always together. You know that Steiner begins to have had enough of Rose; so the husband sticks to him like a plaster lest he should escape.”
"I? If there is anything in the world I really hate, it is going to bed before twelve o'clock at night!" Oh, is that so!
rationales: Outputs 0 and 2 both paraphrase the same specific passage about the contrast between gilded/opulent furniture and second-hand items including mahogany tables and zinc candelabras imitating Florentine bronze. They share identical source content, parallel structure, and similar stylistic choices (e.g., highlighting the 'violent/crude contrast,' the same inventory of furniture items), strongly suggesting they come from the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 0 and 4 share nearly identical stylistic fingerprints: both use 'renowned physician,' 'somber yet determined expression,' 'eyes reflecting [emotion and emotion],' and maintain the same sentence structure and ordering throughout. Key phrases like 'somber yet determined,' 'eyes reflecting,' and the parallel construction of all three sentences strongly indicate they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 3 share nearly identical phrasing and vocabulary: both use 'steeds,' 'porte-cochère,' 'gave a click to the handle, and the door flew open, flinging back a coat of arms adorned with...crowns (a unicorn goring a knight),' and 'capripede caryatid.' The sentence structures are almost the same, with only minor word substitutions (e.g., 'drawn by a pair of' vs. 'towed by two,' 'footman' vs. 'lackey,' 'small crowns' vs. 'little crowns'). These are strong stylistic fingerprints of the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 0 and 3 share very similar stylistic patterns and sentence structures. Both use 'would have liked/much liked to know the name of the hostess/lady,' both use 'recalled Vandeuvres,' both use 'questioning him as to/about the manner in which the English made tea,' and both end with a similar phrasing about interrupting with a question ('as though he had been inwardly following up quite a laborious train of thought' vs 'as if he had been thinking very much while speaking'). These two outputs are clearly the most similar to each other and to the original text's style, suggesting they came from the same paraphraser that stays close to the source text.

Outputs 0 and 3 share several distinctive fingerprints: both use the full name 'Sofia Petrovna Likhutina' (with that spelling), both describe condensation on windows rather than 'sweating panes,' both list characters in the same order (visitors, maid, husband, then Sofia Petrovna herself), and both end with a similar interpretive conclusion about the impossibility/difficulty of the situation rather than a rhetorical question. These stylistic and structural parallels suggest they come from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 3 and 4 share nearly identical phrasing in multiple places: 'The Vaidiyar('s son), who was to accompany him, struggled to control his horse and followed him,' 'those who were going on a long journey should set off/out first,' and 'turned the horse's head towards the road, though with great hesitation.' These distinctive phrase matches, combined with similar sentence-level structures and vocabulary choices, strongly indicate they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 3 both heavily paraphrase the source into a smooth, interpretive narrative style, abandoning the original's literary metaphors and sentence structure. Both restructure the gladiator metaphor away from the opening, use explanatory language ('shielded him from,' 'escape the pressures'), and convert the philosophical statement into descriptive prose. This stylistic fingerprint — interpretive summary rather than close paraphrase — is consistent across both and distinguishes them from the other outputs.

Outputs 3 and 4 share distinctive linguistic fingerprints: both use quotation marks around the descriptor for the garden ('neglected'/'badly kept'), both use the phrase 'not unknown to the reader,' both use 'comrades of the officer'/'officer's comrades,' and both maintain a structure very close to a literal translation of the source. These specific preserved phrases and stylistic choices strongly suggest they came from the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 0 and 3 share the same exclamation 'Ah!', both use the 'plaster' metaphor ('sticks to him like a plaster'), both describe them as 'always together', and both maintain a similar formal/literal translation style. The phrasing around preventing escape is also structurally parallel. These linguistic fingerprints strongly suggest they come from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 3 share nearly identical sentence structure and phrasing: both begin with 'I?', use 'If there was [anything/one thing] in the world I hated', and follow with 'going to bed before twelve' with only minor lexical differences. The overall syntactic pattern and stylistic fingerprint are essentially the same, indicating they came from the same paraphraser.

human_par3: Such was the philosophy that the Plato of Oblomovka had worked out and that lulled him to sleep amidst the stern demands of duty and the problems of human existence! He was not born and educated to be a gladiator for the arena, but a peaceful spectator of the battle; his timid and indolent spirit could not have endured either the anxieties of happiness or the blows inflicted by life – therefore he merely gave expression to one particular aspect of it, and it was no use being sorry or trying to change it or to get more out of it.
llama_31_8b: Oblomov's philosophy allowed him to escape the pressures and responsibilities that came with having a clear sense of duty and purpose. Unlike those born for a life of action and service, he was content to observe from the sidelines. He was not equipped to withstand either the turmoil of great joy or the challenges of life's hardships. As a result, his life was characterized by a lack of drive and initiative, and he did little to achieve his goals, effect change, or seek redemption.

Thus, he embodied an extreme approach to life, never striving, adapting, or expressing remorse.
